log_metadata.purpose: Persistent Codex activity log for all work completed on the AI Project.

update_policy.rule: Update this file after completing any task related to the AI Project.

project_context.high_level_summary: Privacy-first local-first AI orchestration app with service management, multi-tier model routing, A.R.C. prompt orchestration, session transparency, and an integrated semantic memory service backed by Obsidian conversation history.
core_capabilities: Service dashboard for Ollama, Fabric, and Memory
Three-tier routing across local, Haiku, and Sonnet models
Unified chat interface with cost visibility
A.R.C. prompt integration for cloud calls
Semantic retrieval of past conversations through a local RAG service
Observability, history, routing, and transparency tooling
project_context.memory_system_summary: The memory subsystem runs as a local Python FastAPI service on port 8082, ingests Obsidian markdown conversations, stores embeddings in LanceDB, supports hybrid retrieval with HyDE and reranking, and integrates into ARCOS via IPC and UI service controls.
project_context.architecture_summary: ARCOS follows an orchestration pipeline of task classification, prompt strategy selection, context retrieval, prompt construction, model routing, execution, response handling, and storage plus cache, with strong emphasis on transparency, failure handling, and budget governance.

source_documents.note: Filename in workspace uses underscores instead of a dot before prd.

entries.task_title: Initialize Codex project log and absorb project context
entries.summary: Read the core AI Project planning and architecture documents, resolved the requested memory PRD filename to rag_memory_prd.json, summarized the project direction, and created the persistent Codex log file for future task updates.
context_captured: AI Hub PRD v2.0 priorities and routing model
ARCOS WBS phase and task structure
Memory system PRD and WBS progress state
Architecture plan for orchestration, transparency, and storage
entries.next_update_expectation: Append a new entry after each completed AI Project task.

entries.task_title: Package ARCOS for macOS Apple Silicon
entries.summary: Attempted a production macOS build, fixed a packaging-blocking async compile error in the Electron main process, reran the build successfully, and generated fresh 2.1.0 packaged artifacts including the Apple Silicon app bundle and DMG.
notes: Build required running outside the sandbox so Electron could rebuild better-sqlite3.
macOS signing remains disabled because package.json sets mac.identity to null.

entries.task_title: Rename legacy branding to ARCOS
entries.summary: Replaced the prior product branding with ARCOS across the AI Project workspace, including architecture docs, memory docs, Electron product metadata, UI labels, and internal source identifiers such as arcos frontmatter/source labels. Also renamed the architecture plan file to ARCOS Architecture Plan.txt, renamed the app repo folder to arcos, and updated the Electron bundle identifier to com.arcos.
notes: The app repo folder was renamed to arcos to keep workspace paths aligned with the new product name.
Historical package artifacts built before the rename still exist on disk under the prior app name until a new package build is run.

entries.task_title: Draft ARCOS PRD and WBS
entries.summary: Created a new ARCOS product requirements document and work breakdown structure in the AI Project root. Both documents position ARCOS as a PAI-first modular desktop control surface, not a generic chat app, and center the redesign on dockable panels, tab groups, saved layouts, prompt/routing transparency, and explicit system visibility.
notes: The new planning docs treat PAI as the governing system and ARCOS as the visual operating layer.
The WBS is sequenced around a ground-up workspace engine before panel polish or extended features.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS WBS Phase 0
entries.summary: Reviewed the new ARCOS planning documents against the PAI material and the ARCOS architecture plan, confirmed the repo still reflects the older fixed-shell renderer, and resolved the immediate scope mismatch by treating the renderer as a ground-up redesign while preserving backend and service capabilities where practical. Marked Phase 0 complete in the WBS and aligned the log paths with the moved documentation and work-log folders.
notes: The main scope tension was between an existing chat-first renderer and the new panel-based ARCOS direction.
Phase 1 now begins from the current renderer codebase rather than from a blank prototype.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS WBS Phase 1
entries.summary: Built the first real ARCOS workspace foundation in the renderer. Added a persisted workspace state model, panel registry, preset layouts, region resizing, tab groups, collapse/hide behavior, layout reset and restore, and a new workspace shell that replaces the former fixed renderer layout. Integrated the shell with live panels based on the current app surface, including navigator, chat, control deck, models, cost, and utility launchers.
verification: TypeScript check passed with npm exec tsc -- --noEmit
notes: The old modal-only panels remain modal-backed for now and are exposed through the utilities surface until they are converted into true dockable panels in later phases.
This phase establishes the workspace engine so future panel refactors can happen without replacing the shell again.

entries.task_title: Advance ARCOS WBS Phase 2 core panels
entries.summary: Promoted core ARCOS workspace surfaces beyond the initial shell by adding dedicated Services, Routing, Transparency, and Execution panels, while keeping the chat panel stable inside the workspace. Added reusable routing utilities, persisted routing decisions into the routing log from message sends, and introduced a trace store that captures real request and service lifecycle events for the new observability panels.
verification: TypeScript check passed with npm exec tsc -- --noEmit
notes: Phase 2 now includes verbosity tiers and direct panel-link actions from trace entries.
The new observability surfaces are fed by real routing and request events, not placeholder demo data.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS WBS Phase 2
entries.summary: Finished the ARCOS core panel MVP phase. The workspace now has dedicated panels for chat, services, routing, cost, transparency, and execution trace. Added live trace verbosity controls, direct panel links from trace events, and real event capture for chat routing and service lifecycle actions so the new observability surfaces reflect actual runtime behavior.
verification: TypeScript check passed with npm exec tsc -- --noEmit
notes: Phase 2 is now fully checked off in the WBS.
The modal-backed history, memory, and settings surfaces still exist, but the main shell is no longer dependent on the former fixed-layout control structure.

entries.task_title: Add ARCOS Phase 2.5 UI style design to planning docs
entries.summary: Updated the ARCOS planning documents to make the visual redesign explicit as Phase 2.5, positioned between the core panel MVP and the deeper prompt/memory/history panel work. Also repaired the PRD's malformed visual-direction section by turning it into a structured visual system block with reference examples.
notes: Phase 2.5 now explicitly covers visual system definition, workspace restyling, and review against the PRD style references.
The PRD remains valid JSON after the visual-direction fix.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS WBS Phase 2.5
entries.summary: Applied the first dedicated ARCOS visual system pass across the modular workspace. Reworked the palette, typography, panel surfaces, tab styling, controls, navigator, service cards, chat empty state, and key observability panels so the app reads like a serious tool surface instead of an inherited AI chat UI. The restyle uses a restrained dark graphite palette with minimal accents and stronger panel hierarchy, aligned to the PRD's visual references.
verification: TypeScript check passed with npm exec tsc -- --noEmit
notes: This phase focused on the shared visual system and the most visible workspace surfaces rather than every remaining legacy component.
The codebase is ready for you to inspect the new ARCOS visual direction in a running build.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS WBS Phase 3
entries.summary: Promoted prompt inspection, memory, and history into native dockable ARCOS workspace panels. Added a Prompt Inspector that shows current system prompt source, token estimates, current user prompt, and recent conversation context; a docked Memory panel with live search and index status; and a docked History panel for sessions and routing logs. The utilities surface now opens these native panels directly while keeping legacy modal entry points available during transition.
verification: TypeScript check passed with npm exec tsc -- --noEmit
notes: The prompt inspector currently uses estimated token counts and current active prompt composition rather than a full backend-generated final prompt artifact.
Legacy modal surfaces remain available as a fallback while the docked panel flow settles.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS WBS Phase 4
entries.summary: Completed the ARCOS preset and command-surface phase by adding a full preset manager, support for saving the current workspace as custom presets, custom preset duplication, renaming, and deletion, and a richer top-bar preset switcher. Added a global command palette with keyboard-first navigation, panel open and hide commands, preset switching, layout reset, and real service control actions. Also added a panel library that lists all available panels with icons, descriptions, and one-click placement into left, center, or right workspace docks.
verification: TypeScript check passed with npm exec tsc -- --noEmit
notes: Built-in presets now include Command, Coding, Research, and Monitoring, while user-saved presets persist in workspace storage.
The command palette now supports arrow-key selection and Enter-to-run so panel and service actions are genuinely keyboard-first.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS WBS Phase 5
entries.summary: Expanded ARCOS into a true multi-surface workspace by adding detached panel windows backed by Electron, persisted detached panel state, automatic re-docking, and duplicate-prevention between docked and detached panel instances. Added shared panel rendering for docked and detached modes, per-panel error boundaries, recovery controls for broken workspace state, diagnostics for stale or missing panel registrations in persisted layouts, and cross-window workspace synchronization so the main shell and detached windows stay aligned.
verification: TypeScript check passed with npm exec tsc -- --noEmit
Detached panel state now persists through workspace storage and is re-synchronized on bootstrap.
notes: Detached panels open in dedicated Electron windows and can be re-docked from either the detached window or the main workspace header.
Recovery behavior now surfaces panel failures and stale persisted panel ids without collapsing the rest of the ARCOS workspace.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS WBS Phase 6
entries.summary: Pushed ARCOS into a more PAI-native operating layer by adding a dedicated Tools workspace panel for Fabric patterns, plugins, and execution history; a structured Fabric execution store with in-panel progress and output; and richer trace metadata that links routing, services, chat, memory, and tool events back to the correct workspace surfaces. Extended the workspace registry with a first-class tools panel, normalized trace events to include machine-readable panel relationships and entity labels, and connected plugin activation plus memory search and ingest actions into the same observability flow. This phase also clarifies the extension path for future PAI modules: new capabilities now fit naturally into the panel registry, shared workspace panel renderer, detached window path, and structured trace/event model rather than requiring bespoke sidecar UI.
verification: TypeScript check passed with npm exec tsc -- --noEmit
notes: Fabric runs can now be launched, monitored, and stopped from the native Tools panel while also syncing output into the active conversation when available.
Trace and execution panels now use panel-aware links rather than hard-coded source-to-panel assumptions, which gives future PAI surfaces a cleaner integration path.

entries.task_title: Advance ARCOS QA and acceptance validation
entries.summary: Ran the strongest available non-GUI acceptance checks for ARCOS by cleaning the repo to a passing lint state, rerunning TypeScript validation, and producing a fresh packaged directory build for macOS arm64. This confirms that the current modular workspace, detached windows, tools surface, and Electron packaging path all compile and package successfully. Manual live-app acceptance still remains for interaction-heavy checklist items such as panel rearrangement persistence, preset switching behavior across relaunch, in-app request execution visibility, and end-user experience review.
notes: The packaged directory build required escalation so Electron could rebuild better-sqlite3 outside the sandbox.
QA checklist items that require live GUI interaction were intentionally left unclaimed rather than inferred from implementation alone.

entries.task_title: Record manual ARCOS acceptance findings and architecture pivot
entries.summary: Captured the first real user acceptance pass against the current ARCOS build. Manual testing confirmed that layout restore across relaunch works, panel movement/removal/detach/re-dock works, preset switching works, and a real chat request routed correctly with utilities functioning. The same pass also surfaced key product-direction feedback: the current top header is overcrowded and blocks window dragging at default size, presets and panels should move into dropdown menus, the left/center/right region model and collapse behavior should be replaced, the app should open to an empty workspace instead of prebuilt presets, and the desired new panel architecture is a snap-to-grid pocket system inspired by Tarkov stash/raid bag modules with resizable tab containers. Functional failures were also confirmed in the current packaged app: Fabric pattern execution returned a 404 page and ARC-Memory failed to stay running after start/restart attempts.
notes: User likes the current color scheme and font, but noted there is no settings surface yet for changing them.
This acceptance pass should be treated as a pivot point for workspace architecture, not just a bug report against the current shell.

entries.task_title: Rewrite ARCOS planning docs and complete rebuild Phase 1
entries.summary: Rewrote the ARCOS PRD and WBS around the accepted pocket-grid architecture: empty-by-default startup, no built-in presets, dropdown-based workspace controls, remove-not-collapse behavior, and a snapped stash-style grid of module pockets. Then started the rebuild by replacing the old split-region workspace foundation with a new grid-based state model, empty pocket placement flow, sparse draggable top bar, saved layout foundation, and module shells that place real ARCOS panels into a 3x4 default grid. The current app now opens against the new architectural direction instead of the previous left/center/right shell.
notes: Unused split-region management surfaces from the earlier rebuild were removed so the renderer now tracks the new pocket-grid direction more honestly.
Phase 2 of the rebuild is now the snapped module resizing pass.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS rebuild Phase 2
entries.summary: Added snapped pocket resizing to the new grid workspace so modules can grow by whole-cell increments instead of freeform pixels. The new module shell now exposes right-edge, bottom-edge, and corner resize handles that snap against the grid, while the workspace store validates resized bounds and blocks overlap. I also hardened grid row and column adjustments so shrinking the workspace is only allowed when all current modules still fit, keeping the pocket-grid layout predictable and safe.
notes: Phase 2 keeps module resizing snapped to the current row and column grid instead of permitting arbitrary dimensions.
Grid shrink requests now fail safely when placed modules would no longer fit.

entries.task_title: Tighten grid density and complete ARCOS rebuild Phase 3
entries.summary: Adjusted the new grid workspace to better match the denser professional UI direction by reducing module and grid spacing and renaming the empty-state affordance from 'Empty Pocket' to the user-preferred 'empty module' label. Also closed the saved-layout phase: ARCOS now uses the `Layouts` dropdown as the real replacement for the old preset system, with save, rename, duplicate, delete, reset-to-empty, and relaunch restore all flowing through the new grid-based workspace state.
notes: The graphite theme and font direction were preserved while tightening spacing to better fit the Pro Tools-like density preference.
Phase 4 is now the core module migration pass inside the new grid shell.

entries.task_title: Flatten module seams and close ARCOS rebuild Phase 4
entries.summary: Tightened the pocket-grid workspace again to remove the remaining empty space between modules and reduce visual framing to thin seam lines instead of thick card edges. The grid now renders as a denser continuous surface with pixel seams, flatter module headers, and simpler empty-module cells while preserving the approved graphite color system and typography. I also audited the current panel registry and renderer wiring, confirmed the core ARCOS/PAI surfaces are already seated in the new grid shell, and marked Phase 4 complete in the rebuild WBS.
notes: The workspace now uses tight pixel seams between modules instead of visible gutter spacing.
Phase 5 is now the next rebuild step: advanced behavior on top of the grid architecture.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS rebuild Phase 5
entries.summary: Completed the advanced behavior pass for the pocket-grid rebuild by auditing the surviving detached-window and recovery systems, then hardening the workspace store around grid-era failure modes. Persisted workspace hydration now sanitizes and rewrites invalid live and saved layouts, removes duplicate docked-versus-detached panel state, drops overlapping or out-of-bounds module placements with explicit diagnostics, syncs repaired detached-panel state back to Electron, and surfaces a clear warning when re-docking stops because the grid is full. This preserves detached windows, re-dock behavior, panel failure isolation, and stale-layout recovery without reintroducing the old split-region assumptions.
notes: Phase 6 is now the next rebuild step: theme hooks and experience controls.
The detached-window and panel-error-boundary infrastructure was already present; this phase focused on making the grid-era persistence and recovery behavior explicit and safe.

entries.task_title: Complete ARCOS rebuild Phase 6
entries.summary: Added real appearance controls to ARCOS by extending the persisted settings model with theme and font selection, exposing those controls through a new Appearance tab in Settings, and applying them at the renderer root through CSS-driven semantic tokens. The default graphite theme remains intact, but ARCOS now supports alternate slate and oxide palettes plus selectable type voices, all without breaking the dense control-surface direction. I also used the phase to tighten the product language in the chat, navigator, settings, and panel registry so ARCOS reads more clearly as a PAI operating surface rather than a generic AI chat shell.
notes: Graphite remains the default ARCOS theme, matching the accepted palette direction.
The next step after the rebuild phases is the QA and acceptance pass, plus the open Fabric and ARC-Memory bug track.

entries.task_title: Apply QA-driven workspace interaction and appearance revisions
entries.summary: Used the first post-rebuild QA pass to tighten ARCOS around the accepted interaction model. The pocket-grid shell now supports snapped module dragging by header, top and left anchored resizing in addition to the original bottom-right growth, and quieter empty cells with no plus-label treatment. I also replaced the placeholder appearance card buttons with compact dropdown-driven controls, added native system font enumeration from macOS font directories, and expanded appearance settings to include default-plus-media themes, font selection, text color, accent color, and secondary accent color. This pass is intentionally logged as a QA refinement pass rather than a completed acceptance phase, because the new behavior still needs a live retest in the packaged app.
notes: System font choices are sourced from standard macOS font directories and applied directly as CSS font-family values.
Acceptance remains in progress pending a fresh manual pass on movement and appearance behavior.

entries.task_title: Simplify ARCOS font picker to fixed curated set
entries.summary: Removed the native system-font enumeration bridge and simplified ARCOS appearance font selection to a fixed curated list that matches the accepted product direction. The settings panel now offers IBM Plex Sans, Arial, Menlo, Futura, and Papyrus, while the rest of the appearance system remains intact. This keeps font choice intentional, reduces moving parts, and avoids the failed system-font workflow from the previous QA pass.
notes: Menlo was chosen as the terminal/plain-text font option.
Appearance theme and color controls were left unchanged.

entries.task_title: Fix packaged ARC-Memory startup resources
entries.summary: Completed bug-track item B.2 by fixing the packaged ARC-Memory startup path. The packaged app had been trying to launch ARC-Memory from process resources, but the memory-service directory was not being bundled into the app at all, which caused startup to return and then immediately fall back to stopped. The packaging config now includes memory-service as an extra resource, excluding Python cache artifacts, and the main process startup path now validates that the bundled directory exists and launches the declared arc-serve entrypoint from that resource directory. Rebuilt the packaged directory app and confirmed that ARCOS.app now contains Contents/Resources/memory-service.
notes: This fixes the packaged-resource cause of the ARC-Memory start-then-stop failure.
B.1 remains intentionally out of focus at the user's request, even though the Fabric CLI fallback work is already in place.

entries.task_title: Connect ARC-Memory to ArcVault and enable generic Obsidian ingest
entries.summary: Connected ARC-Memory to the Obsidian vault at /Users/noahpowell/ArcVault by adding vault configuration to both the source memory-service and the packaged ARCOS memory-service resource, installing the required nomic-embed-text Ollama embedding model, and relaxing the Obsidian reader so plain markdown notes are indexed as source_type=obsidian instead of being rejected for missing AI-export frontmatter. Also applied the same ingest-reader change to the packaged ARCOS app resource so a service restart picks it up, then started the first full-force ingest against the ArcVault corpus.
verification: Forced ingest accepted 1144 markdown files from /Users/noahpowell/ArcVault and began embedding them into LanceDB
notes: Before this change, ARC-Memory was pointed at the vault path but indexed zero files because the reader only accepted notes with source frontmatter set to chatgpt, claude, or arcos.
The running ARC-Memory process must be restarted once to load the updated packaged reader and .env configuration.

entries.task_title: Clean up ARC-Memory result presentation for Obsidian vault search
entries.summary: Adjusted ARC-Memory and the ARCOS memory UI so Obsidian search behaves like note search rather than raw chunk debugging. The memory reader now skips empty-note bodies during ingest, the server query path filters placeholder results and deduplicates hits by source_path, and the renderer now labels Obsidian explicitly while replacing the misleading negative percentage score display with simple ranked result positions. Rebuilt the packaged ARCOS app so the updated memory panel behavior is available in the shipped bundle.
notes: The running ARC-Memory service must be restarted once to load the rebuilt packaged server code.
Existing stale empty-note rows are suppressed at query time; future ingests also skip empty notes at the reader level.

entries.task_title: Close ARCOS rebuild QA and acceptance phase
entries.summary: Marked the ARCOS rebuild QA phase complete in the WBS based on the packaged-app acceptance passes already completed: empty-workspace startup, module add/remove flow, snapped resizing, layout save-and-restore, draggable title bar behavior, core PAI module operation inside the pocket-grid shell, and the follow-up memory result cleanup pass. The rebuild track is now complete, with the remaining Fabric 404 issue preserved as a separate deferred bug-track item rather than a blocker on the rebuild phase set.
notes: This closes the planned rebuild phases through QA/acceptance.
B.1 Fabric remains open by choice and is not part of the completed rebuild acceptance set.

entries.task_title: Refresh stale memory planning docs and align architecture plan with PAI execution chain
entries.summary: Rewrote the memory PRD and memory WBS so they reflect the current ARC-Memory implementation rather than the original bootstrap assumptions. The updated docs now describe broad ArcVault ingestion, general Obsidian note support, implemented ARCOS panel/service integration, and the real remaining gaps: /memory composer integration, ARCOS write-back, and formal validation work. I also rewrote the ARCOS Architecture Plan to make the intended execution path explicit: ARCOS app -> user prompt -> PAI core context -> OpenClaw -> Fabric -> prompt rebuilder -> local model, with supporting systems and fallback behavior described around that main chain.
notes: The prior architecture plan described a more generic orchestration pipeline and did not clearly state the OpenClaw -> Fabric -> prompt rebuilder -> local model chain.
The new memory WBS is now a remaining-work document rather than a stale initial-build checklist.

entries.task_title: Consolidate planning docs into one active PRD and one active WBS
entries.summary: Collapsed the documentation set into a single active ARCOS PRD, a single active ARCOS WBS, and the ARCOS architecture plan as the governing reference. The new PRD now combines the valid parts of the old ARCOS, AI Hub, and memory planning work around the canonical PAI execution chain, while the new WBS separates completed foundations from the real remaining work. Removed the stale assistant, memory, and gameplan documents from the documentation folder so only active planning artifacts remain.
verification: documentation folder reduced to ARCOS Architecture Plan.txt, arcos_prd.json, and arcos_wbs.md
notes: The architecture plan remains the main driving document and is now referenced directly by the unified WBS.
The unified PRD includes remaining work items such as Fabric reliability, memory composer integration, write-back, layout import/export, local model management, and observability depth.

entries.task_title: Complete unified WBS Phase 5 — Fabric reliability and chain role
entries.summary: Completed the first remaining phase from the unified ARCOS WBS by tightening Fabric execution reliability and making Fabric-stage participation explicit in the UI. The main-process Fabric runner now tries multiple server route shapes before falling back cleanly to the Fabric CLI, eliminating the lingering 404-style packaged failure path. I also added explicit Fabric-stage metadata to the trace model, surfaced execution mode and stage labels in the Tools panel, and updated trace/execution panels so Fabric now reads as a visible workflow stage in the PAI chain rather than just a generic tool run.
notes: Fabric execution now reports whether it ran through the server path or CLI fallback.
This phase improved chain visibility, but it did not yet move memory into the composer or implement write-back.

entries.task_title: Complete unified WBS Phase 6 — OpenClaw runtime integration
entries.summary: Inserted an explicit OpenClaw phase into the unified ARCOS plan and implemented the first real OpenClaw integration slice in the app. ARCOS now treats OpenClaw as a first-class service in the runtime panel, inspects the live ~/.openclaw configuration boundary to determine gateway state and paths, and exposes direct entry points to the OpenClaw control UI, workspace, logs, and config without cloning the OpenClaw codebase into ARCOS.
notes: OpenClaw is integrated by reference from ~/.openclaw rather than cloned into the ARCOS repo.
This phase adds runtime visibility and launch points, not full prompt-path handoff through OpenClaw.

entries.task_title: Fold claw-code-parity architectural patterns into the unified ARCOS WBS
entries.summary: Reviewed the claw-code-parity roadmap and parity docs to identify the parts that materially improve ARCOS architecture rather than just its terminal implementation details. Added concrete WBS work for explicit execution lifecycle states, typed event schemas, normalized failure taxonomy, structured recovery policies, degraded-mode reporting, and future coding-runtime awareness so ARCOS can mature into a machine-readable control plane instead of depending on log-shaped state.
notes: The useful carryover from claw-code-parity is event/state architecture, not tmux or TUI-specific mechanics.
Branch/worktree awareness was added as future coding-runtime metadata rather than immediate core UI work.

entries.task_title: Complete unified WBS Phase 7 — memory in active prompt composition
entries.summary: Implemented `/memory` directly in the ARCOS message composer. The composer now runs ARC-Memory searches from `/memory <query>`, shows inline loading and error states, stages retrieved citations above the input, supports per-citation removal and direct Obsidian opening, and injects the retained memory context into the next outgoing prompt without changing the user-visible message text.
notes: The composer flow is intentionally two-step in this phase: `/memory <query>` stages citations, and the next normal send includes them.
Offline ARC-Memory now degrades inline instead of failing silently.

entries.task_title: Implement ARCOS conversation write-back into the Obsidian vault
entries.summary: Connected the existing vault-write path to the active ARCOS conversation flow so completed assistant turns now write the current conversation back into ArcVault automatically. The vault writer now uses stable conversation-keyed filenames, richer frontmatter including conversation_id, updated_at, and message_count, and the chat layer records write-back success or failure in trace so the memory loop is visible rather than silent.
notes: Implementation for write-back is complete, but WBS item 8.3 remains pending until a live run confirms ARC-Memory watcher ingestion of a newly written ARCOS note.
Manual sidebar save-to-vault continues to work through the same writer path.

entries.task_title: Fix embedding-model misrouting in local ARCOS chat
entries.summary: Patched ARCOS so embedding-only Ollama models such as nomic-embed-text are no longer treated as valid chat targets. The installed model list is now filtered to chat-capable models, auto-selection prefers known chat defaults, and the chat service now recovers at send time by switching away from an embedding-only saved model before making an Ollama request.
notes: This fix unblocked the Phase 8 write-back QA pass after ARCOS attempted to chat with nomic-embed-text and received an Ollama 400 error.
The packaged app was rebuilt after the fix.

entries.task_title: Fix Obsidian citation URI generation for ARC-Memory results
entries.summary: Patched ARC-Memory citation generation so Obsidian links are built from the configured VAULT_PATH instead of a hardcoded vault name, and file targets are URL-encoded correctly. This fixes retrieval-result citations for notes whose paths include folders, spaces, or other characters that were previously passed through as raw strings.
notes: The packaged app was rebuilt so the bundled memory-service includes the URI fix.
Phase 9.2 remains pending until you retest citation opening in the rebuilt app.

entries.task_title: Verify ARCOS vault-note ingestion and searchability
entries.summary: Verified that ARCOS-written notes under /Users/noahpowell/ArcVault/arcos are valid memory inputs and become searchable once ARC-Memory ingests them. The two ARCOS notes were present on disk but initially absent from search results because the index had not yet incorporated them; after triggering an ingest, the index advanced from 1086 to 1088 docs and live queries for "say test number 1" and "dogwater" returned the ARCOS notes as top results.
verification: curl POST /ingest followed by curl GET /status
notes: This confirms WBS items 8.3 and 9.1.
The remaining open memory-loop QA item is 9.2, citation opening in the rebuilt app.

entries.task_title: Complete unified WBS Phase 9 — layout portability and local model management
entries.summary: Implemented the remaining Phase 9 portability and operations work in ARCOS. Workspace layouts can now be exported and imported through native file dialogs with sanitizer-backed validation, and the model-management surface now includes installed-model metadata such as disk usage, family, parameter size, quantization, active model state, and recommendation notes while preserving in-app pull and delete actions.
notes: Imported layouts are passed through the same workspace sanitizer as persisted state, so invalid or overlapping module placements are repaired before activation.
The package build includes the new layout-transfer IPC and the richer Ollama metadata view.

entries.task_title: Complete unified WBS Phase 10 — observability depth
entries.summary: Completed the observability-depth phase by adding typed execution-state metadata, failure taxonomy, and recovery guidance to ARCOS trace events; strengthening the trace and history panels with machine-grounded summaries, routing confidence, estimated costs, and saved learnings visibility; and adding focused ARC-Memory tests for note normalization, dedupe, result filtering, and Obsidian citation URI generation.
verification: uv run python -m unittest discover -s tests
notes: The new unit tests caught a real citation-path edge case in ARC-Memory and that helper was fixed before the phase was closed.
This phase also closes WBS item 9.2 because citation opening was retested successfully against the configured vault path.

entries.task_title: Complete unified WBS Phase 11 — extensibility
entries.summary: Completed the extensibility phase by formalizing plugin manifests around PAI architecture roles, execution boundaries, target stages, and entry surfaces, and by adding a dedicated Runtime panel that exposes structured repo/worktree state, merge readiness, stale-branch signals, and verification scope without relying on raw terminal output.
verification: uv run python -m unittest discover -s tests
notes: The Runtime panel resolves the linked OpenClaw workspace from ~/.openclaw while surfacing structured git metadata for the active repository when ARCOS is running in development.
Per the push cadence, this phase should be pushed to GitHub after completion.

entries.task_title: Correct ARCOS chat path to enforce the canonical execution chain
entries.summary: Reworked the chat hot path so requests no longer jump directly from the composer to the model stream. ARCOS now runs each request through a staged canonical chain that emits real-time transparency and execution entries for user prompt intake, PAI core context assembly, OpenClaw context loading, Fabric-stage evaluation, prompt rebuilding, and only then model dispatch.
notes: The OpenClaw stage currently loads curated workspace context from ~/.openclaw/workspace while the live service state still gates whether the stage is healthy or degraded.

The Fabric stage is now always represented in the chain, but without a default transformation pattern it currently acts as an explicit shaping checkpoint with pass-through behavior rather than silently disappearing.

entries.task_title: Transition ARCOS planning from phased buildout to product refinement
entries.summary: Updated the ARCOS WBS to mark the major build program as structurally complete and established a standing Product Refinement track for incremental tuning. This changes the planning model from large rebuild phases to ongoing small fixes and behavior polish, while keeping Codex_Log as the continuing execution record.
notes: The phased ARCOS build program is now treated as complete at the structural level.
Future ARCOS changes should be tracked under Product Refinement unless the architecture changes materially.

entries.task_title: Add full OpenClaw and Fabric chain integration to ARCOS refinement plan
entries.summary: Updated the ARCOS WBS refinement section to include the missing chain-integration work: real OpenClaw request handoff, an OpenClaw boot/runtime bring-up script, active Fabric skill selection for normal chat requests, explicit execution-path distinctions, and end-to-end chain validation.
notes: This captures the gap between the current staged chain and the intended full OpenClaw-plus-Fabric execution path.
The next implementation work should come from the new 18.x refinement items.

entries.task_title: Define ARCOS-specific OpenClaw bootstrap and runtime contract
entries.summary: Reworked the OpenClaw workspace from a generic first-run bot scaffold into an ARCOS-focused runtime sidecar. Updated the architecture plan, PRD, and WBS to keep PAI CORE canonical, added an ARCOS-specific bootstrap, runtime contract, and hook/event contract under ~/.openclaw/workspace, and added an ARCOS boot-validation script in the repo for future runtime bring-up.
notes: SOUL.md, IDENTITY.md, and USER.md were left intact as the human-facing identity layer and not duplicated into the new runtime contract.
The remaining implementation gap is real OpenClaw request handoff and active Fabric selection, which stay open under Product Refinement 18.5-18.9.

entries.task_title: Increase OpenClaw agent timeout to five minutes
entries.summary: Raised the OpenClaw agent model timeout budget from the observed 60-second idle limit to 300000 ms so slower local-model responses have up to five minutes before timing out.
notes: Applied both idleTimeoutMs and requestTimeoutMs at the default agent-model level and the active per-model entry for ollama/qwen3:8b.
OpenClaw schema was not locally discoverable, so the change was aligned to the exact failure mode reported in logs: LLM idle timeout (60s).

entries.task_title: Add GitHub-safe OpenClaw onboarding templates and setup docs
entries.summary: Added a repo-owned OpenClaw template workspace, a sanitized example openclaw config, and setup documentation so anyone cloning ARCOS can understand the external OpenClaw and Fabric requirements without exposing the live local config.
notes: The template intentionally excludes the real ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json and any secrets or machine-specific credentials.
The README was rewritten to reflect ARCOS as a PAI control plane with OpenClaw and Fabric as required external runtime dependencies.

entries.task_title: Implement real OpenClaw runtime handoff in ARCOS chat chain
entries.summary: Replaced the placeholder OpenClaw file-load stage with a real gateway handoff. ARCOS now opens a token-authenticated WebSocket connection to the local OpenClaw gateway, submits a per-request orchestration analysis through chat.send, waits for completion, reads the resulting session analysis, and feeds that output back into prompt rebuilding, transparency, and routing-tier override logic.
notes: OpenClaw now materially affects the chain by returning structured orchestration output and an optional routing-tier recommendation.
Fabric is still passive in normal chat. The remaining gaps are 18.6 through 18.9: active Fabric selection, explicit path distinctions, and end-to-end validation.

entries.task_title: Activate Fabric execution inside the normal ARCOS chat chain
entries.summary: Made Fabric active during normal chat when the OpenClaw stage selects a pattern. ARCOS now executes the suggested Fabric pattern during the canonical chain, records the result in transparency/execution, and injects the Fabric output into prompt rebuilding instead of treating Fabric as pass-through only.
notes: This completes the runtime step where OpenClaw can select a Fabric pattern and ARCOS will actually execute it during the chat chain.
The remaining refinement items are 18.7 through 18.9: stronger selection logic, clearer path distinction in UI, and end-to-end validation with captured stage outputs.

entries.task_title: Expose explicit execution-path labels in ARCOS transparency surfaces
entries.summary: Added structured chain-path metadata to the trace model and surfaced it in the Transparency, Execution, and Routing panels. ARCOS now explicitly labels whether a request used direct pass-through, OpenClaw-only, OpenClaw-plus-Fabric, or degraded fallback instead of requiring the user to infer the path from scattered stage messages.
notes: This closes the UI distinction gap between runtime stages and the overall chain path taken by a request.
The remaining open item in this refinement block is 18.9: end-to-end validation with captured stage outputs, plus stronger Fabric selection logic under 18.7 if needed.

entries.task_title: Add a reusable ARCOS chain validation harness and identify the live OpenClaw blocker
entries.summary: Added a repo-local validation harness intended to exercise the real ARCOS chain through OpenClaw, Fabric, prompt rebuilding, and the local model. The harness revealed that the current OpenClaw gateway integration is not yet operational for write-capable ARCOS calls: direct WebSocket attempts, the installed OpenClaw gateway helper, and CLI gateway calls all close before `chat.send` completes.
notes: The live failure is `gateway closed (1006 abnormal closure (no close frame))` against `ws://127.0.0.1:18789` before `chat.send` completes.
OpenClaw method-scope metadata confirms `chat.send` and `agent.wait` require `operator.write`, while `chat.history` only requires `operator.read`.
The current OpenClaw config also contains unsupported timeout keys under `agents.defaults.model` and `agents.defaults.models.ollama/qwen3:8b`; OpenClaw now flags those as invalid.
Result: 18.9 remains open. The next fix is to establish a valid ARCOS/OpenClaw operator-write auth path and clean up the OpenClaw config schema.

entries.task_title: Complete live ARCOS chain validation and capture the remaining Fabric integration gap
entries.summary: Corrected the OpenClaw config schema, fixed the chain-validation harness to use the working local gateway path, and completed end-to-end validation through OpenClaw analysis, prompt rebuilding, and local model dispatch. The captured results show the remaining gap clearly: the chain now degrades at the Fabric stage because OpenClaw is selecting patterns that are not present in the local Fabric install.
notes: The validator now reaches the live OpenClaw gateway successfully; the earlier 1006 failures were caused by the sandboxed/origin-invalid harness path rather than a persistent runtime auth failure.
Two real cases were captured end-to-end. Both degraded at the Fabric stage because the selected patterns `code_review` and `prompt_rebuilder` are not installed locally.
Result: refinement item 18.9 is complete. The next real integration task is 18.7: improve Fabric selection and map OpenClaw-selected intents to installed Fabric patterns.

entries.task_title: Implement installed-pattern validation and Fabric intent mapping for the ARCOS chain
entries.summary: Hardened the Fabric selection stage so ARCOS no longer attempts to execute invented pattern names. OpenClaw now receives the installed Fabric pattern list and is asked to return either an exact installed pattern or a conceptual fabric_intent, and ARCOS resolves the result through exact-match, alias, and keyword-overlap mapping before execution.
notes: The chain now distinguishes between exact installed-pattern selection, mapped selection, and unresolved Fabric requests in transparency and execution surfaces.

This closes refinement item 18.7 at the application level. The next useful live check is to verify real ARCOS prompts now resolve OpenClaw Fabric requests onto installed local patterns such as `review_code` or `improve_prompt` instead of degrading on nonexistent names.

entries.task_title: Fall back to Ollama when Claude is not configured
entries.summary: Patched the ARCOS send path so a missing Claude API key no longer hard-blocks messages when Ollama is available. If routing or OpenClaw recommends a cloud tier but Claude credentials are absent, ARCOS now falls back to the local Ollama tier and records that override in the routing reason.
notes: Selecting an Ollama model was never enough to force local execution while routing mode remained auto; the bug was that ARCOS still attempted a Claude tier and surfaced the missing-key error.
ARCOS now degrades to local execution instead of failing the send when Claude is unconfigured and Ollama is running.

entries.task_title: Persist per-message chain artifacts for durable ARCOS request inspection
entries.summary: Added a durable chain-capture path so each ARCOS message can write a structured artifact to disk with the routing decision, OpenClaw analysis, Fabric resolution, rebuilt prompts, and final dispatch result. This closes the gap where transparency data was visible only in-memory and not inspectable after the request finished.
notes: Artifacts are written under ~/.noah-ai-hub/history/chains/YYYY-MM/ and include both stage diagnostics and the final dispatch outcome for each saved message.
The next live check is to send one new ARCOS message and then inspect the newly created chain artifact to verify OpenClaw analysis and Fabric resolution for that exact request.

entries.task_title: Unify ARCOS onto the validated OpenClaw gateway path and persist OpenClaw failure reasons
entries.summary: Replaced the app-only custom OpenClaw WebSocket client with the same local CLI gateway call path that already worked in validation. ARCOS now uses a single proven OpenClaw transport path, and saved chain artifacts now include the OpenClaw failure reason when analysis is missing.
notes: This removes the mismatch where validation used one OpenClaw path but the app used another.
The next live check is to resend the same code-review prompt and inspect the new chain artifact for actual OpenClaw analysis and Fabric selection data.

entries.task_title: Fix OpenClaw main-process blocking regression that caused ARCOS beachballing
entries.summary: Replaced the synchronous OpenClaw gateway subprocess calls with asynchronous subprocess execution in the Electron main process. This removes the UI-thread blockage that caused ARCOS to beachball when sending prompts through the unified OpenClaw path.
notes: The regression was caused by spawnSync-based OpenClaw gateway calls in the Electron main process, which blocked the app event loop during analysis.
ARCOS now uses the same unified OpenClaw path without freezing the UI.

entries.task_title: Force ARCOS Fabric execution to use the active local model and longer Ollama timeout
entries.summary: Patched the Fabric execution path so ARCOS no longer relies on Fabric's misconfigured local defaults. ARCOS now passes the active selected Ollama model into Fabric runs and sets a longer Ollama HTTP timeout for the Fabric CLI fallback, which removes the immediate bad-default-model failure mode from the ARCOS chain.
notes: The Fabric CLI fallback now invokes fabric with the current ARCOS-selected Ollama model instead of inheriting Fabric's standalone DEFAULT_MODEL setting.
ARCOS also forces OLLAMA_HTTP_TIMEOUT=300s for the Fabric child process so slow local model startup does not fail at Fabric's shorter default timeout.

entries.task_title: Convert prompt rebuilder behavior into Response Composer preservation mode
entries.summary: Changed the final chain stage to behave as a Response Composer rather than a second full reasoning pass. ARCOS now instructs Fabric-backed requests to preserve Fabric as the authoritative intermediate result and reformat it into the PAI response structure instead of re-deriving the answer from scratch.
notes: The visible stage label is now Response Composer in trace language and architecture documentation, while stored field names remain stable for compatibility.
The main behavioral change is that Fabric-backed requests now carry explicit preservation instructions so the final model stage should format strong upstream output instead of diluting it.
